title: Fixing Type Inference for a Generic Math Calculator

statement.markdown: Given a generic math calculator class `Calculator<T>` with methods `add`, `subtract`, and `multiply`, implement the class to correctly infer the return type of the methods based on the generic type `T`. The calculator should support addition, subtraction, and multiplication of two numbers of type `T`, where `T` can be either `number` or `string`. The calculator should also handle cases where the user attempts to add a `number` and a `string`.
statement.inputFormat: Input is a string representing a mathematical expression in the format 'a op b', where 'a' and 'b' are operands and 'op' is the operation (+, -, *).
statement.outputFormat: Return the result of the operation as a string.
statement.notes: The calculator should correctly handle cases where the operands are of type `number` or `string`, and the operation is valid for the types of the operands.

constraints.description: Maximum length of the input string

examples.explanation: Addition of a number and a string

hints.text: Use the `unknown` type to handle cases where the user attempts to add a `number` and a `string`.
Use the `typeof` operator to determine the type of the operands.

editorial.title: Editorial Title
editorial.intuition: The correct approach is to use the `unknown` type to handle cases where the user attempts to add a `number` and a `string`, and then use the `typeof` operator to determine the type of the operands.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would be to check the types of the operands manually, but this would be inefficient and prone to errors.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution would be to use the `unknown` type and the `typeof` operator to handle the cases automatically.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution would be to use a combination of the `unknown` type and the `typeof` operator to handle all cases correctly and efficiently.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness is based on the fact that the `unknown` type can handle any type, and the `typeof` operator can determine the type of any operand.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run would involve testing the code with different inputs to ensure that it handles all cases correctly.
commonMistakes: Using the `any` type instead of the `unknown` type
Not using the `typeof` operator to determine the type of the operands

starterCode.javascript: class Calculator<T> { add(a: T, b: T): string { return String(a + b); } subtract(a: T, b: T): string { return String(a - b); } multiply(a: T, b: T): string { return String(a * b); } }

buggyStarterCode.javascript: class Calculator<T> { add(a: T, b: T): string { if (typeof a === 'number' && typeof b === 'number') { return String(a + b); } else { return 'Invalid operation'; } } subtract(a: T, b: T): string { if (typeof a === 'number' && typeof b === 'number') { return String(a - b); } else { return 'Invalid operation'; } } multiply(a: T, b: T): string { if (typeof a === 'number' && typeof b === 'number') { return String(a * b); } else { return 'Invalid operation'; } } }
